results.possibleIdentity: Philip Kemp Meany (1931–2014) was an American artist, educator, and editor active from the late 1950s through the 1990s. Based in Duluth, Minnesota, he served as an Associate Professor in the School of Fine Arts at the University of Minnesota Duluth (UMD) for 35 years, where he taught drawing, silk screen printing, and design before retiring in 1996. Meany was a significant figure in the experimental and independent art scenes of the 1960s and 1970s; he was the founder and editor of "Generative Press" and was an active participant in the international Mail Art movement, with his work documented in the Lomholt Mail Art Archive. His professional network included prominent avant-garde figures such as Dick Higgins and Ken Friedman, and he participated in conferences like the Visual Studies Workshop's "Options in Independent Art Publishing." Meany held an MFA from the University of Iowa (1956) and was a Fulbright scholar in Florence, Italy. In his later years, he collaborated with his wife, textile artist Janet Meany, providing design and technical assistance for her publication, "The Weavers Friend."

Robert Knisel was a highly active master printer and technical collaborator at the renowned print publisher Gemini G.E.L. (Graphic Editions Limited) in Los Angeles during the late 1960s and throughout the 1970s. He is best known for his technical collaborations with major 20th-century artists, including Sam Francis, Frank Stella, Keith Sonnier, Andy Warhol, Robert Rauschenberg, Ellsworth Kelly, and Bruce Nauman. His work often involved complex screenprinting and offset lithography techniques. Knisel is specifically associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) through his role as a printer for the 'New York Collection for Stockholm' portfolio (1973), which was a major E.A.T. project organized to donate works to the Moderna Museet in Sweden. He also worked closely with Gary Reams at their own 'Knisel-Reams Studio' and was a key figure in the production of Keith Sonnier's technologically driven 'Video Still Screen' series and 'Air to Air' multimedia projects. His contributions are documented in the collections of major institutions such as the National Gallery of Art and the National Gallery of Australia.

Robert A. Knisel (1920–2010) was an American painter and arts administrator based in Florida. A World War II veteran and former executive in the textile industry at J.P. Stevens in New York City, he retired to Panama City, Florida, in 1982. He became a prominent figure in the local art community, serving as the president of the Panama Art Association. He was instrumental in finalizing plans for the building of the Visual Arts Center (VAC) in Panama City, where he also taught oil painting and frequently exhibited his own works in center art shows. While he was active as an artist during the late 1960s and 1970s through his painting practice, his primary professional career during that time was in the textile industry in New York.

results.possibleIdentity: Jack Ward was an electronic engineer and technical staff member at Bell Laboratories (Bell Labs), where he was active from the early 1970s (starting in 1972) until 2004. Identifying as a "Visual Communicator," Ward worked at the intersection of technology and visual media, a focus that aligns with the mission of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization. He is noted for his interest in how technology, such as the transistor, can be used for artistic and communicative purposes, often emphasizing that human brains are more naturally "wired for pictures" than for words. In later years, he became a prominent historian of semiconductor technology, founding the Transistor Museum (and its accompanying website) to preserve and document the early history of electronics, including oral histories of pioneering engineers and scientists from Bell Labs and RCA who were contemporaries of the E.A.T. movement.

Jack Ward was a California-based sculptor and artist active during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He was part of a circle of West Coast artists who experimented with new materials and environmental forms. Ward is recorded as having participated in significant group exhibitions alongside prominent figures like Sam Richardson and Gurdon Woods. Notable exhibitions include "Color" at the City of Palo Alto in 1971 and the "1st Annual Sculpture Invitational" in 1973. His work during this era frequently involved the use of industrial or synthetic materials, which was a hallmark of the collaborations between artists and engineers promoted by the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization during that period.

Jack Ward was the founder of Jack Ward Color Service, a prominent professional color photography laboratory located in Manhattan (New York City). Active from 1955 until 2012, the business was a central service for artists, photographers, and advertising professionals during the late 1960s and early 1970s. Ward provided technical support, custom photo processing, and duplicate transparencies for numerous artists and high-fashion photographers, including Art Kane. Given E.A.T.'s base in New York and its focus on providing technical services and professional collaborations to artists, Ward and his lab functioned as a key technical resource within the city's artistic and technological landscape.

results.possibleIdentity: Tony Cohan (born December 28, 1939) is an American author, lyricist, and musician whose career spans several decades, with significant activity beginning in the late 1960s. He is associated with the experimental art and technology movements of that era, most notably through his contribution to Gene Youngblood’s seminal 1970 book 'Expanded Cinema', which is a foundational text regarding the intersection of technology and art—a focus shared by the organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). During this period, Cohan worked as a journalist for the underground newspaper 'Los Angeles Free Press' and served as the creative director at Capitol Records (1970–1971). His background as a musician includes playing jazz drums in Europe during the early 1960s with figures such as Dexter Gordon and Bud Powell. He later became a prominent lyricist, collaborating with jazz pianist Chick Corea on projects like the classic 'High Wire: The Aerialist'. In 1975, he founded Acrobat Books, an independent press dedicated to the arts. Cohan is also widely recognized for his travel literature and novels, including the 1981 New York Times Notable Book 'Canary' and his popular memoir 'On Mexican Time' (2001), which details his life in San Miguel de Allende, Mexico.

results.possibleIdentity: Michael Jon "Mickey" Dvortcsak (born 1938, died 2019) was a California-based artist whose career spanned over five decades, beginning in the 1960s. He earned his Bachelor of Arts in 1961 and his Master of Fine Arts in 1968 from the University of California, Santa Barbara (UCSB), where he studied under Howard Warshaw and Rico Lebrun. Dvortcsak was deeply engaged in the intersection of art and science; in 1967, he published an article titled "The Artist/Scientist in the Twentieth Century" in the Harvard Art Review. His work from this period, such as the mixed-media mural "Christ Invests Himself Organically with the Very Majesty of the Universe" (1967), sought to synthesize spiritual, scientific, and artistic inquiries, drawing inspiration from figures like Buckminster Fuller, Albert Einstein, and Pierre Teilhard de Chardin. This focus aligns with the goals of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement, which promoted collaborations between artists and scientists during the late 1960s and early 1970s. Dvortcsak taught at UCSB for five years before becoming a full-time artist in the mid-1970s. His work, which evolved from abstract expressionism to figurative and metaphorical landscapes, has been exhibited at the Santa Barbara Museum of Art, the San Francisco Museum of Art, and the Pennsylvania Academy of Art. His paintings are held in permanent collections including the Museum of Contemporary Art (MOCA) in Los Angeles and the Walker Art Center in Minneapolis.
